mnemonics.title: GREEDY
mnemonics.content: GREEDY stands for: G - Grab best local choice, R - Right now (make decision immediately), E - Every step is optimal, E - Extend solution, D - Don't look back (no backtracking), Y - Yes to immediate benefit. This reminds you that greedy algorithms make locally optimal choices at each step hoping for global optimum.

mnemonics.title: A S K F O R H E L P
mnemonics.content: Activity Selection Key Facts Of Greedy: A - Activities sorted by finish time, S - Select first activity, K - Keep selecting non-overlapping activities, F - Forward check, O - Optimal by greedy choice, R - Recursive implementation, H - Horizon (deadline), E - Earliest finish time, L - Late start, P - Proof by exchange argument.

mnemonics.title: KNAPSACK
mnemonics.content: Knapsack Key: K - Keep ratio (value/weight), N - N items available, A - Add highest ratio first (Fractional), S - Select O-Optimal for fractional, P - Partition problem (0/1), A - ApprOximate for 0/1, C - Consider dynamic approach for 0/1, K - Know it's greedy for fractional only.

mnemonics.title: HUFFMAN CODE
mnemonics.content: Huffman Coding: H - Heap (min-heap for frequencies), U - Use priority queue, F - Build tree bottom-up, F - Frequent = short code, M - Merge two smallest, A - Assign 0/1 left/right, N - No code is prefix of another.

mnemonics.title: DIJKSTRA
mnemonics.content: Dijkstra's Algorithm: D - Distance array, I - Initialize source to 0, J - Join set (visited), K - Keep finding minimum, S - Set distances, T - Track parent, R - Relax edges, A - Add to SPT (Shortest Path Tree).

mnemonics.title: MST Methods
mnemonics.content: Prim's vs Kruskal's: PRIM'S - P - Start from one vertex, R - Grow one tree, I - Include nearest vertex, M - Minimum edge to tree, S - Spans all vertices. KRUSKAL'S - K - Keep sorting edges, R - Rank/sort by weight, U - Union-Find data structure, S - Select minimum edge, K - Kruskal's method, A - Add if no cycle, L - List all edges.

acronyms.usage: Use when a globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal choice. Check this property first before applying greedy.
Use when an optimal solution to the problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Both GCP and OSP must hold for greedy to work.
Use when connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight. Apply Prim's (O(V²) or O(E log V)) or Kruskal's (O(E log V)).
Use Dijkstra's to build SPT from single source to all vertices. Remember: works for non-negative weights only.
Use with Kruskal's algorithm to detect cycles. Implements Find and Union operations, often with path compression and union by rank.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a horizontal timeline with vertical bars representing activities. Start from left, pick the first finishing activity, then jump to the next non-overlapping one. Think of it like jumping across stepping stones where each stone ends before the next begins.

Picture a weighing scale with different items (gold dust, water, coins). Since items are divisible, you fill the knapsack by taking highest value-to-weight ratio items first. Visualize pouring liquid into a container - you always pour the most valuable first.

Think of a binary tree growing from bottom. Start with leaf nodes (characters), merge two smallest into a parent node repeatedly. Each merge adds a bit (0 for left, 1 for right). Final codes are read from root to leaves.

Imagine ripples spreading from a stone dropped in water. Each ripple reaches new nodes at distances proportional to edge weights. Keep a "visited" set (inner circle) and "unvisited" (outer area). Always process the nearest unvisited node first.

Picture a tree growing from a seed vertex. At each step, one new branch (minimum edge) connects to the tree. Think of a spider web or roots growing - always extending from existing structure with shortest connection.

Imagine all edges as separate sticks on a table. You pick the shortest stick that doesn't create a cycle (forms a forest). Keep picking and connecting until one tree forms. Think of building a bridge network piece by piece.

analogies.analogy: Like choosing the tallest person in each row to form a basketball team without checking if they'll play well together. You make the best immediate choice without considering future consequences - works sometimes, fails sometimes.

Like scheduling exam slots in a university. To maximize number of exams, schedule the exam that finishes earliest, then the next that doesn't overlap, similar to fitting maximum number of meetings in a day.

Like packing for a trip with a weight limit. You can take partial items - if you have 5kg allowance and 10kg of gold (very valuable) and 8kg of clothes (less valuable), you'd take all gold and some clothes, filling the bag optimally.
Like Morse code assignment - more frequent letters (E, T) get shorter codes (dot, dash), rare letters (Q, Z) get longer codes. This minimizes total message length.
Like using Google Maps for shortest route. It explores paths progressively, always updating when a shorter path is found, but only works when all road distances are positive (like no negative tolls).

Like connecting all cities with minimum total road length. Whether using Prim's (grow from one city) or Kruskal's (pick shortest roads anywhere), you want the cheapest network that reaches all cities.
Like a freelancer accepting projects. Take the highest paying job that fits before its deadline, then move to next. Skip low-paying jobs to make room for better ones - always pick maximum profit.
